title: Chapter 21: Dreams of the Abyss
content: The entity came for Ren in his sleep.

He'd been expecting it. Since the garden confrontation with the possessed Feng and the tea house incident with Hong, the Salvation System had been monitoring his sleep patterns for signs of dimensional intrusion. The entity couldn't manifest physically in the human world—not yet—but it could reach through micro-fractures in reality, finding minds that resonated at frequencies it could exploit.
Ren's mind resonated at a frequency the entity knew intimately: the War God frequency. The vibration of a warrior who'd spent six years fighting the Abyss and lost.
The dream started in the Himalayas.

He was standing on a ridge—cold, thin air, the world spread below him in whites and grays. The sky was wrong. Torn. Areas of the atmosphere had been replaced by *nothing*—not darkness, not void, but an absence so complete that looking at it made his eyes water and his thoughts stutter.
Below the ridge, an army was forming.

Not human. Not anymore. The soldiers who'd held this position in the previous timeline—the last seven—were there, but *changed*. Their skin was translucent, their eyes gold, their movements synchronized with a precision that no human group could achieve.
Because they weren't human anymore. They were the entity's collection—warriors consumed and repurposed, their skills preserved, their identities dissolved.

"You remember this." The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Not Feng's voice, not Hong's voice—it was the entity itself, speaking with the layered resonance of a mind that existed in multiple dimensions simultaneously. "The Battle of the Eastern Gate. Your last stand. The place where everything ended."
"I remember." Ren kept his voice steady. In dreams, the entity had limited influence—it couldn't force action, couldn't compel speech. It could only show and suggest. "I also remember winning."
"Winning?" The entity's amusement was palpable—a vibration that made the torn sky ripple. "You survived. Six of your companions did not. Seven billion humans did not. You call that winning?"
"I call it not losing. Which is the same thing, in a war of attrition."

"Such a soldier." The entity's awareness moved closer—not physically, but conceptually, a shadow folding across the dreamscape like a lens bending light. "I've consumed warriors from seventeen dimensions, War God. Generals, champions, god-killers. You're not the most powerful I've faced. But you are the most... persistent."
"Flattery won't work."

"It's not flattery. It's an offer." The dreamscape shifted—the Himalayas dissolving, replaced by a vision of Crystal City. But not the Crystal City Ren knew. This was Crystal City *transformed*—towers of crystalline light reaching into a sky that shimmered with dimensional energy, human figures moving through the streets with grace and purpose, their eyes gold but not vacant, their movements coordinated but not enslaved.

"The future the Patriarch envisions," the entity said. "Not consumption—*evolution*. Humanity merged with the infinite consciousness, retaining individuality while accessing the knowledge of seventeen dimensions. No more war. No more disease. No more death. The limitations of your species—gone."
"Show me the cost."

The vision shifted again. This time, the streets were empty. The towers stood, but the light was gone. Human figures remained—but frozen, mid-motion, their faces locked in expressions of surprise or horror or understanding. Statues in a city of monuments.

"Every evolution has casualties," the entity said. "The ones who can't adapt. The ones who resist. But the ones who *embrace* the change—they live forever. They become the architecture of a new reality. Is that not worth the sacrifice of those too rigid to bend?"
"You're showing me a sales pitch."

"I'm showing you *truth*. The same truth the Patriarch sees. The same truth your companion Liu Haoran saw—briefly—before he died and came back... differently." A pause. "Did he tell you what he saw on the other side, War God? Did he tell you why he came back *changed*?"
Ren went still. Liu Haoran had never fully described his experience of death and return. The man was ally, informer, and mystery—helpful but distant, reliable but strange.

"He saw the evolution from the inside," the entity continued. "And part of him *accepted* it. That's why he came back differently—because he's not entirely on your side anymore. No one who's seen what lies beyond the breach can be entirely on your side. The truth is too vast for your small war."
"You're lying."

"I am *showing*. Whether you believe what you see is your choice." The dreamscape began to dissolve, the frozen city fading back into the darkness of sleep. "But consider this, War God: the Patriarch believes he can control the opening. Liu Haoran believes he can navigate it. The Zhao elder believed he could profit from it. Everyone who approaches the breach believes they're the exception—the one who won't be consumed."
"And you're different?"

"I am the breach. I am what waits on the other side. And I am telling you—sincerely, with no deception—that the only way to *truly* protect the people you love is not to seal the breach, but to *embrace* it. Let them evolve. Let them become more than human. Let them survive the only way survival is possible in a multiverse that will not wait for your species to be ready."
"That's not survival. That's surrender."

"Then you'll die. And the people you're trying to save will die with you. Not because I kill them—because the multiverse doesn't care about your defiance. Dimensions collapse. Realities end. The only permanent survival is transcendence."
The dream ended.
Ren woke up gasping, his hand reaching for a weapon that wasn't there. The room was dark, silent, ordinary. The Salvation System's interface flickered in his vision:

`DREAM INCURSION DETECTED`
`SOURCE: Abyss Main Consciousness (The Waiter in the Dark)`
`TYPE: Psychological manipulation — not combat, persuasion`
`CONTENT: Offer of "evolution" in exchange for compliance`
`THREAT LEVEL: Low (physical) — High (psychological)`
`NOTE: The entity's claims about Liu Haoran cannot be verified. This may be disinformation designed to sow distrust between allies.`
*Liu Haoran.*
Ren sat up in bed, heart pounding. The entity had planted a seed of doubt—precisely the kind of seed that grew in darkness, feeding on uncertainty and isolation.
*Is Liu compromised? Did he see something on the other side that changed him?*
He couldn't know. Not without asking, and asking would reveal that the entity had gotten to him.

*That's the play,* Ren realized. *The entity tells me Liu is compromised so I'll push him away. Without Liu, I lose the outside intelligence network. Without the network, I'm blind. And a blind soldier makes mistakes.*

It was a classic intelligence tactic: isolate the target from allies by poisoning trust. The War God had seen it a hundred times in the previous timeline—entities whispering doubts, teammates turning on each other, units collapsing from within because someone believed a lie about someone else.
But recognizing the tactic didn't make the doubt vanish. What if Liu *was* compromised? What if the entity was telling the truth about him and lying about everything else?
*The most dangerous lies are the ones wrapped in truth.*
Ren forced himself to breathe. To think. To apply the discipline that had kept him alive through six years of war and one reincarnation.
*Fact: Liu Haoran has provided accurate intelligence. Every lead he's given has checked out. The Bronze Bell, the Tokyo retrieval, the Node 4 frequency coordinates—all verified.*

*Fact: The entity has every reason to sow distrust. If Liu is compromised, the entity wouldn't need to tell me—it would simply use him. The fact that it's *warning* me suggests Liu is still a threat.*
*Conclusion: Liu is more likely an asset than a liability. The entity is trying to neutralize him through me.*
The logic was sound. But the doubt—the seed—was already planted.
*I'll verify,* he decided. *Not by asking Liu directly, but by testing. Small deceptions fed to different channels. If the entity knows things only Liu should know, I'll have my answer.*
He lay back down and stared at the ceiling until dawn.
That morning, Feng brought breakfast to his room.

It was such a domestic, unremarkable gesture that Ren didn't immediately recognize it for what it was: a signal. Feng had never visited Ren's room. Never brought him food. Never acknowledged his existence outside of family settings.
The tray contained tea, congee, and a folded note beneath the bowl.
Ren waited until Feng left to read it.

*"Father is planning a demonstration for Zhao Kang in 48 days. A controlled aperture. Small-scale. He needs two bloodline channelers for the ritual—himself and me. But my infection makes me unreliable. He's considering using Xue instead."*
Using Xue.
For a blood sacrifice ritual.

Ren's blood ran cold. A "small-scale aperture" was still a breach—a tear in reality, even a controlled one, would require a bloodline channeler to power the dimensional frequency. And channeling a dimensional breach was lethal.
The Patriarch was going to sacrifice his daughter to prove his project worked to the Zhao elder.

Not because he wanted to—because he *needed* to. Zhao Kang's withdrawal had deprived him of funding and infrastructure. He needed the demonstration to bring the Zhao family back. And Feng was too compromised to channel safely.
Which meant Xue was the only available bloodline channeler who could power the aperture without exploding.
*He's going to kill her.*
The Salvation System agreed:

`THREAT ASSESSMENT: CRITICAL`
`If the demonstration proceeds with Xue as channeler, she will not survive the ritual. Dimensional channeling at the required intensity is fatal without either: (1) War God-level power to buffer the energy, or (2) fully converted nodes providing stabilized resonance.`
`CURRENT STATUS: The Patriarch has 4 converted nodes providing partial stabilization, but the aperture will still be lethal to any channeler below master-level capability.`
`Xue's channeling level: Intermediate (12 days of training). NOWHERE NEAR sufficient.`
`DEADLINE: 48 days to prevent the demonstration.`
Forty-eight days.
Ren folded the note and burned it over his tea candle.
*Step one: Get more powerful. Step two: Learn enough about the demonstration's array to sabotage it. Step three: Make sure Xue is never in that room when the aperture opens.*
His phone buzzed. Liu Haoran.
*"The entity visited you last night. I felt the resonance from across the city. What did it say?"*
Ren typed: *"It told me you're compromised."*
A long pause. Then:

*"I wondered when it would try that. I'm not compromised, Zhou. But I am... different. I saw things when I died. Things I can't unsee. Some of them were beautiful. Some of them were true. The entity uses truth as a weapon—but that doesn't make the truth less true."*
*"What truth?"*

*"The multiverse is larger than your war, War God. The breach the Patriarch is opening isn't the only door. It's not even the only door on this planet. There are seven dimensional weak points on Earth, and the Liang family's estate sits on one of them. The Patriarch can be stopped here—but if the other six weak points aren't sealed, another patriarch, another entity, another apocalypse will simply arrive through a different door."*
Seven weak points. One per continent, if the dimensional geometry followed the pattern Ren had seen in the previous timeline.
*The war is bigger than this family. Bigger than this city.*
He'd suspected it. Now he knew.
*"I'll seal this breach first,"* he typed. *"Then we'll talk about the other six."*
*"Fair enough. But Zhou—the demonstration in 48 days. I have information about the array configuration. Meet me at the old tea house. Tomorrow, 10 PM."*
*"Hong's tea house?"*
*"New location. I'll send coordinates."*
*"And Liu—"* Ren hesitated. *"Thank you for being honest about what you saw."*
*"Don't thank me. Just remember: the entity's lies are built on truth. The danger isn't that you'll believe the lies. The danger is that you'll stop believing the truth."*
One thousand sixty-nine days.
